title: The Invisible Gorilla: How Our Intuitions Deceive Us
author: Christopher Chabris, Daniel Simons
excerpt: The famous 'invisible gorilla' experiment revealed something startling: we massively overestimate what we notice and remember. This book explores six everyday illusions that distort our perception, memory, and judgment, showing why we are not the reliable observers we think we are.
keyIdeas: Inattentional blindness causes us to miss obvious things when our attention is focused elsewhere
We suffer from an illusion of attention, believing we notice more than we actually do
Memory is reconstructive, not reproductive—we confabulate without realizing it
Confidence is not a reliable indicator of accuracy
keyTakeaways: Don't trust your intuitions about what you've noticed or remember
Be skeptical of confident eyewitness testimony
The feeling of knowing is not the same as actual knowledge
Correlation does not imply causation—the illusion of cause is powerful
whoShouldRead: Anyone interested in the limits of human perception and memory
Professionals who rely on their observations (investigators, doctors, journalists)
Students of psychology and cognitive science
Trial lawyers and anyone working with eyewitness evidence
whoShouldSkip: Readers who find cognitive dissonance uncomfortable
Those looking for practical self-help rather than fascinating research
